\Section{Specification Inference for Higher-Order Code}
\label{sec:validation}

\SubSection{Spec Inference Overview}
\label{sec:validation-overview}

\MVP's \emph{specification inference} based on weakest-precondition (\WP) analysis~\cite{dijkstra1975wp} was extended to handle function-valued code.
\WP inference takes arbitrary Move code and, for each function, computes conditions that are both necessary and sufficient for the implementation's behavior.
The \WP of a higher-order function is expressed in terms of \texttt{ensures\_of} and \texttt{aborts\_of} on its function-valued arguments rather than inlining their bodies; without this modular abstraction, inferred specs would be impractically large.
This extension serves two purposes, demonstrated in the following subsections: reducing the annotation burden on developers writing higher-order specs (Sec.~\ref{sec:validation-amm}), and providing a validation pipeline that confirms the encoding is internally consistent (Sec.~\ref{sec:validation-tests}).

\SubSection{AMM Compliance Case Study}
\label{sec:validation-amm}

Recall from Sec.~\ref{sec:amm} that the !Pool! struct carries a !pricing! closure constrained by a no-abort invariant: the pricing function must not abort for any inputs in any state.
Two concrete pricing implementations were shown: !constant_product_with_fee!, which is compliant (it never aborts), and !with_fee_non_compliant!, which is not (it aborts when the owner's !Fee! resource is absent).
Spec inference makes this compliance distinction machine-readable without any manual annotation.

\Paragraph{Bottom-up: exposing abort behavior}
Given the implementation of !with_fee_non_compliant! with no written spec, \WP inference derives its abort conditions automatically:

% @formatter:off
\begin{MoveBox}
  spec with_fee_non_compliant { // [inferred]
    aborts_if !exists<Fee>(owner);
    aborts_if Fee[owner].bps > 10000;
    aborts_if reserve_in + effective_in == 0;
    ...
  }
\end{MoveBox}
% @formatter:on

\noindent The inferred abort condition --- that the function aborts when the !Fee! resource for the owner is absent --- directly contradicts the Pool no-abort invariant, which requires that !self.pricing! never aborts for any inputs in any state.
When this function is used to construct a \texttt{Pool}, \MVP reports a pack-time invariant violation --- a precise, counterexample-driven diagnosis of non-compliance.

\Paragraph{Constructor-side: compliance as a conditional postcondition}
Running \WP inference on !create_pool!, which accepts an arbitrary !pricing! argument, makes the compliance obligation explicit as a guard in the postcondition:

% @formatter:off
\begin{MoveBox}
  spec create_pool { // [inferred]
    ensures (forall x: u64, y: u64, z: u64:
               !aborts_of<pricing>(x, y, z))
        ==> publish<Pool>(addr, Pool{..., pricing});
  }
\end{MoveBox}
% @formatter:on

\noindent The pool is published only when the guard holds, i.e.\ only when !pricing! satisfies the no-abort invariant.
For the non-compliant constructor, the guard can never be satisfied, so the desired postcondition is vacuous --- the pool is never published.
For the compliant constructor, the guard always holds and the pool is reliably created.
In both cases, spec inference makes the compliance condition machine-readable without any manual annotation.

\SubSection{Validation Pipeline and Test Coverage}
\label{sec:validation-tests}

Inferred conditions are labeled \texttt{[inferred]} and feed directly back into \MVP for verification, creating a pipeline: code $\leadsto$ code+specs $\leadsto$ VC, with verification expected to succeed.
Tab.~\ref{tab:inference-tests} summarizes the inference test suite exercising behavioral predicates across all HOF patterns introduced in this paper.
All categories pass end-to-end, confirming that the encoding of behavioral predicates and state labels is internally consistent and that inferred specifications are tight enough for the verifier to discharge.

\begin{WideTable}
    \caption{Inference test categories that exercise behavioral predicates.}
    \label{tab:inference-tests}
    \centering
    \small
    \begin{tabularx}{\linewidth}{l X}
      \hline
      \textbf{Category} & \textbf{Representative cases} \\
      \hline
      Direct calls &
        Single and chained calls to a spec'd helper; inferred specs use
        \texttt{ensures\_of} and \texttt{aborts\_of} to abstract over the
        callee's effect rather than inlining its body. \\
      \hline
      Mutual recursion &
        Mutually recursive \texttt{is\_even}/\texttt{is\_odd}; inference
        breaks the cycle by phrasing each spec in terms of the other's
        behavioral predicate. \\
      \hline
      Higher-order parameters &
        A function that takes a closure and applies it; the inferred spec
        abstracts the closure via \texttt{ensures\_of}/\texttt{aborts\_of}
        on the parameter, with no commitment to a particular implementation. \\
      \hline
      Stored-closure dispatch (enum) &
        Calculator with an enum variant carrying a closure field;
        dispatch through the variant is summarised by behavioral
        predicates on that field. \\
      \hline
      Stored-closure dispatch (struct field) &
        Vault with a \texttt{Strategy} closure stored in a struct field on
        Aptos framework types; inference emits \texttt{modifies\_of} for
        the field together with the field-form behavioral predicates. \\
      \hline
      Pre/post state labels &
        \texttt{move\_to}, \texttt{move\_from}, mutable resource indexing;
        inferred specs distinguish \texttt{@pre} from post-state on the
        memory the predicate observes. \\
      \hline
      Intermediate state labels &
        Sequenced state-modifying calls (e.g.\ remove-then-publish);
        inferred specs introduce existentially quantified intermediate
        labels and pin them to defining behavioral predicates. \\
      \hline
    \end{tabularx}
\end{WideTable}
